skill.name: event-store-design
skill.description: Design event stores for event-sourced systems, including storage schemas, technology choices, concurrency controls, subscriptions, and replay patterns.
skill.summary: Guides event store design for PostgreSQL, EventStoreDB, DynamoDB, and related event-sourced architectures.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: AI review downgraded the static risk score after checking SKILL.md. The backtick, weak cryptography, reconnaissance, and fetch detections are false positives from markdown fences, documentation text, schema examples, database calls, and public reference links. One medium issue remains because the EventStoreDB example disables TLS for a localhost URI, which needs a publication warning for production use.

medium_findings.title: Example EventStoreDB Connection Disables TLS
medium_findings.description: The EventStoreDB template shows a connection URI with tls=false. This is reasonable for a local development endpoint, but users could copy it into production and send event data over an unencrypted connection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The insecure setting is explicit, but the URI targets localhost. The risk depends on whether users adapt the example for non-local deployments without changing TLS settings.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The Ruby/shell backtick detections point to markdown code fence delimiters around diagrams and examples, not executable shell commands. No evidence found of command execution logic in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are markdown fence delimiters or boundaries of fenced examples. They are not interpreted as Ruby backticks or shell execution by the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Network Detections Are Database Examples And Documentation Links
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for exfiltration. The fetch detections are asyncpg database reads inside an illustrative Python template, and the URLs are public documentation resources. No evidence found of hidden outbound requests, credential transfer, or unauthorized data collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The method calls are SQL client calls on a database connection, not browser fetch or HTTP exfiltration. The hardcoded URLs are visible reference links at the end of the document.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Text Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography detections occur in ordinary documentation text such as the skill name, description, headings, and table labels. No cryptographic implementation or weak algorithm usage was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is natural language related to design and descriptions. There is no crypto API, cipher selection, hashing function, or key handling code at these locations.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Detections Are Schema And Example Identifiers
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system reconnaissance detections map to SQL column names, index names, UUID imports, and query placeholders in event store examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, process inspection, or environment reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows data model fields and parameterized database examples. None of the locations include commands that inspect the local system or collect host metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: Example EventStoreDB Connection Disables TLS
dangerous_patterns.description: The EventStoreDB template shows a connection URI with tls=false. This is reasonable for a local development endpoint, but users could copy it into production and send event data over an unencrypted connection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The insecure setting is explicit, but the URI targets localhost. The risk depends on whether users adapt the example for non-local deployments without changing TLS settings.

content.user_title: Design Event Stores for Event-Sourced Systems
content.value_statement: Event-sourced systems need durable event storage, ordering, concurrency, and replay support. This skill provides architecture guidance and templates for PostgreSQL, EventStoreDB, and DynamoDB event stores.
actual_capabilities: Explains event store architecture, stream ordering, global positions, and subscriptions.
Compares EventStoreDB, PostgreSQL, Kafka, DynamoDB, and Marten for event sourcing.
Provides PostgreSQL schema templates for events, snapshots, checkpoints, and indexes.
Drafts Python examples for appending, reading, and subscribing to events.
Shows EventStoreDB and DynamoDB usage patterns for event persistence.
limitations: Provides illustrative templates; production systems need security, migration, and operational review.
Does not run migrations or provision databases by itself.
Does not validate event schemas or guarantee concurrency correctness in every database.
Example connection settings may need TLS, authentication, and cloud-specific hardening.

use_cases.title: Design a New Event Store
use_cases.description: Plan streams, global ordering, snapshots, indexes, and subscription checkpoints for a new event-sourced service.

use_cases.title: Choose Event Storage Technology
use_cases.description: Compare PostgreSQL, EventStoreDB, Kafka, DynamoDB, and Marten against team skills and workload requirements.

use_cases.title: Review Persistence Patterns
use_cases.description: Check an event store design for concurrency, idempotency, replay, backpressure, and operational concerns.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Technology
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose an event store technology for a new event-sourced service. Compare PostgreSQL, EventStoreDB, Kafka, DynamoDB, and Marten for my workload.

prompt_templates.title: Design a PostgreSQL Event Store
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PostgreSQL event store for my aggregate streams. Include tables, indexes, optimistic concurrency, snapshots, and subscription checkpoints.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Existing Event Store
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this event store design for correctness and operational risk. Focus on ordering, idempotency, replay, schema evolution, and backpressure.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Scaling and Recovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a scaling and disaster recovery plan for a high-throughput event store. Cover partitioning, projections, retention, backups, replay, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: I need an event store for an order service on PostgreSQL.
output: Recommended tables for events, snapshots, and subscription checkpoints.
Indexes for stream reads, global reads, event type lookup, and time-based operations.
Concurrency guidance using expected stream versions and idempotent event identifiers.

output_examples.input: Should I use Kafka as my event store?
output: A comparison of Kafka against event-store requirements.
Guidance on when Kafka fits streaming workloads and when per-aggregate reads become difficult.
Alternative options for durable event sourcing with replay and stream version checks.

output_examples.input: Review my event store before launch.
output: A checklist covering append-only writes, ordering, snapshots, subscriptions, and backpressure.
Production concerns for TLS, authentication, backups, retention, and observability.
Follow-up questions about expected throughput, replay needs, and failure recovery objectives.

best_practices: Define stream IDs, event metadata, and versioning rules before writing production events.
Use optimistic concurrency and idempotent event identifiers for every append path.
Plan snapshots, subscription checkpoints, backups, replay, and monitoring as part of the initial design.
anti_patterns: Treating events as mutable records that can be edited after publication.
Copying local development connection settings into production without TLS and authentication.
Using one global event stream for every query without stream-specific indexes or projections.

faq.question: What does this skill help design?
faq.answer: It helps design event stores for event-sourced systems, including schemas, streams, snapshots, subscriptions, and replay patterns.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: It provides templates and design guidance. Production code still needs security review, testing, migrations, and environment-specific configuration.

faq.question: Which technologies does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers EventStoreDB, PostgreSQL, Kafka, DynamoDB, and Marten at a design and template level.

faq.question: Does it handle event schema evolution?
faq.answer: It advises event versioning from the start, but teams must define compatibility rules and migration processes.

faq.question: Is it useful for cloud deployments?
faq.answer: Yes, especially for DynamoDB and managed database planning. Cloud-specific security and capacity settings still require review.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
